pi_items.source: Code analysis of python generator in setup_task.sh
Code analysis: Sum of rooms defined in python generator script is 80 King + 70 Double + 50 Deluxe + 20 Suite = 220.
Date arithmetic for Q3 2024 (July 31, Aug 31, Sept 30). Also explicitly confirmed by python script looping range(92).
Found exactly matching output path inside export_result.sh and verifier.py logic.

Code execution: The python generator script has a logical bug (dividing individual room_count by 5 and selecting only 5 rows per day), yielding an actual daily occupancy mathematically constrained between 7.73% and 25.00%.
Code execution: Evaluating the synthetic data generation script produced daily average rates ranging roughly $128 to $204, confirming it falls within the claimed [120, 250] range.
Code execution: Due to the extremely low overall occupancy bug in the data generator (~16.35%), the RevPAR values range around $15 to $33 per day, entirely contradicting the [80, 200] claim.

privileged_info_summary: The task operates on synthetically generated hotel Property Management System (PMS) data created dynamically via a Python script in setup_task.sh. I verified the hotel has exactly 220 rooms (80 King, 70 Double, 50 Deluxe, 20 Suite), Q3 2024 spans exactly 92 days, and the expected output path is /home/ga/Documents/Spreadsheets/hotel_revenue_analysis.xlsx. However, I discovered a critical logical bug in the data generation script: because it only loops 5 times total per day (once per rate code) and calculates 'sold' as a fraction of a single randomly chosen room type rather than the whole hotel, it only generates 5 under-sold records per day. While the resulting Average Daily Rate (ADR) of ~$147 correctly falls within the claimed [120, 250] USD tolerance range, the overall occupancy averages only ~16.35% (contradicting the claimed [60, 95] percentage range), and the resulting RevPAR averages ~$24.09 (contradicting the claimed [80, 200] USD range). Additionally, the verifier entirely fails to check these numerical tolerances.
